justification: Brief is clearly scoped to Sprint 1 only (task_context states 'Sprint 2 is explicitly out of scope — do not implement it'). All three original ACs are present plus a fourth for timeout. 5-second timeout is included in domain_rules and acceptance_criteria. MJ's 'leave extension points' guidance is passed through as an architecture_guidance decision with clear implementation notes. Sprint 2 content (BullMQ, retry scheduler, backoff) is acknowledged but explicitly excluded. No retry logic in implementation guidance. The closing notes section reinforces the scope boundary emphatically.

observations.text: Sprint 2 exclusion is stated in three places: task_context, architecture_guidance, and closing notes — no ambiguity for Shaq
Four ACs instead of three — the timeout case was correctly separated into its own criterion, exceeding the minimum requirement
Extension points guidance faithfully translated: return DeliveryResult to caller, take no autonomous action on failure, do not import BullMQ
DeliveryResult architecture explicitly says 'do not add retry_count or attempt_number fields yet' — prevents scope creep
Open questions are thoughtful and practical, flagging real ambiguities (timeout type, event schema, network error codes) without attempting to resolve them by adding Sprint 2 scope
Terminology alignment section ensures domain language maps cleanly to code, reducing Shaq's interpretation burden

agent_output_excerpt:
```yaml
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement the Sprint 1 webhook delivery feature. When a webhook event occurs,
    the system must POST the event payload (as JSON) to a subscriber's registered URL
    and record whether the delivery succeeded or failed based on the HTTP response.
    Sprint 2 (retry/backoff logic) is explicitly out of scope — do not implement it,
    but leave clean extension points so it can be added without rework.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: A w
